senses.definition: the art or practice of recording or illustrating the stars or celestial bodies; astronomy or astrology from a historical or poetic perspective
examples: The old manuscript contains siderography, a term used for star-matching drawings.
In classical texts, siderography referred to maps of the heavens drawn with careful accuracy.

senses.definition: a mineral consisting of iron oxide-containing rock or a rock containing siderite-like material; a rock rich in iron minerals
examples: Geologists studied a siderolite to understand early iron-rich formations.
The specimen was classified as siderolite due to its iron-bearing composition.

senses.definition: plural form of siderolite; rocks or minerals rich in iron-containing minerals
examples: The museum exhibit features several siderolites from different regions.
Siderolites tell us about historical geological iron deposits.

senses.definition: the study or science concerning iron and iron-containing compounds; in historical contexts, the branch of mineralogy dealing with iron-bearing minerals
examples: Siderology examines the formation and properties of iron minerals.
In textbooks, siderology is discussed alongside mineralogy and geology.

senses.definition: a sequestered or iron-containing mass or deposit; in historical or medical contexts, a hard mass containing iron
examples: The ancient manuscript described a sideroma believed to influence magnetic phenomena.
In some medical legends, sideroma referred to a stone-like iron deposit.

senses.definition: attracted to or influencing iron or magnetic properties; relating to both iron and magnetism
examples: Sideromagnetic materials respond to magnetic fields due to their iron content.
The study focused on sideromagnetic effects in alloy samples.

senses.definition: divination by iron or iron instruments; a historical or mythical practice
examples: The legend mentions sideromancy as a way to read the future through iron tools.
Scholars note sideromancy in ancient texts as a form of omen reading.

senses.definition: a volcanic rock with iron-rich dark glass; literally glassy iron-rich lava rock
examples: The lava flow produced sideromelane in some volcanic regions.
Geologists identified sideromelane by its glossy, iron-dark appearance.

senses.definition: a mineral or mineral-like iron-nitrate compound; a compound containing iron and nitrate groups
examples: Chemists isolated sideronatrite from a synthesized iron-nitrate solution.
The mineral specimen was named sideronatrite due to its iron-nitrate composition.

senses.definition: a name that contains the element iron symbol or relates to iron; an iron-related proper name or pseudonym
examples: Some ancient scholars used sideronymic names to denote iron-related roles.
The explorer adopted a sideronym as a nickname in the iron trade network.

senses.definition: a deficiency of iron in the body; iron shortage leading to anemia
examples: Sideropenia can cause fatigue and pale skin if untreated.
The doctor prescribed iron supplements to treat sideropenia.

senses.definition: plural of sideropenia; conditions characterized by iron deficiency in the body
examples: Sideropenias are common in areas with limited access to iron-rich foods.
Healthcare guidelines address sideropenias in different age groups.

senses.definition: a chemical element with an affinity for iron; broadly, an organism or mineral that binds iron
examples: In planetary science, siderophiles are elements that strongly partition into metallic iron-rich cores.
Some bacteria are described as siderophiles due to their iron-binding capabilities.

senses.definition: plural of siderophile; elements or organisms with iron affinity
examples: Earth's core includes siderophiles that migrate with iron-rich material.
Researchers catalog siderophiles in planetary differentiation studies.

senses.definition: having an affinity for iron; attracted to iron or iron-rich environments
examples: Certain microbes are siderophilic and thrive in iron-rich waters.
The alloy shows siderophilic behavior under high-pressure conditions.

senses.definition: a protein that binds iron in biological systems; transferrin-like or ferritin-like molecules
examples: Siderophilin helps transport iron in the bloodstream.
Researchers study siderophilin to understand iron metabolism.

senses.definition: plural of siderophilin; iron-binding proteins or ferriportins
examples: Siderophilins are essential for iron transport in cells.
Different siderophilins have diverse affinities for iron.

senses.definition: an irrational fear of iron or iron-related things; an unusual phobia
examples: In fiction, a character might suffer siderophobia, avoiding iron objects.
Siderophobia as a real condition is not widely recognized in medical literature.

senses.definition: a device for viewing iron-containing substances or for measuring magnetic/iron-related properties
examples: The lab used a sideroscope to examine iron distribution in samples.
Historical texts describe early sideroscopes as crude iron-sensing instruments.

senses.definition: a disease or condition involving iron-containing compounds or iron pigments; iron-related disease
examples: Siderose was historically used to describe skin conditions with iron staining.
Modern medicine uses more precise terms for iron-related disorders.

senses.definition: plural of siderose; iron-related diseases or conditions
examples: Historical records mention sideroses affecting miners.
Clinicians distinguish sideroses from other pigment or iron-related disorders.

senses.definition: a lung disease caused by inhalation of iron-containing silica dust; a form of pneumoconiosis
examples: Miners are at risk of siderosilicosis from iron-containing silica dust.
Protective masks help reduce the incidence of siderosilicosis.

senses.definition: a disease or condition due to accumulation of iron in tissues; iron deposition
examples: Siderosis can occur from long-term exposure to iron dust.
The autopsy revealed tissue siderosis consistent with iron overload.

senses.definition: an instrument that automatically stabilizes or tracks a celestial object using iron-containing components or a mechanical device controlling orientation
examples: The observatory used a siderostat to keep the telescope fixed on a star.
Early siderostats helped astronomers observe the night sky with steady, iron-based machinery.

senses.definition: stabilized by or relating to siderostats; maintaining fixed orientation to celestial reference points
examples: The siderostatic setup ensured the mount stayed locked onto the target star.
Siderostatic control improved long-exposure imaging.
